paper_title: Revisiting Turner Window Axions: The Untapped Potential of NaI Dark Matter Detectors

perturbations.why_wrong: The text states that $g_{aNN}^3$ is fixed to be positive, but then claims that $g^0_{aNN} > 0$ corresponds to 'same signs' and $g^0_{aNN} < 0$ to 'opposite signs'. This is mathematically inconsistent with the standard definition of relative signs.

perturbations.original:
For the QCD axion, either the KSVZ axion of Eqs. (\ref{eq:KSVZ}) or the DFSZ axion of Eqs. (\ref{eq:DFSZ}),
$g_{aNN}^0$ and $g_{aNN}^3$ are both negative, and thus
these axion models are confined to the right panel.  In the case of the KSVZ
axion, there is a single track which can be parameterized in terms of the KSVZ axion mass.
For the DFSZ axion, there are multipole tracks depending on the mixing angle
$\beta$. The limiting tracks, $\sin^2\beta$ = 0 and 1, are
shown, defining a band that must be fully probed to exclude this axion. For  $\sin^2{\beta}=1$, the SNO constraint is quite restrictive, 
while the photon yield from KII is marginal, never exceeding 15 events.
For $\sin^2{\beta}=0$, the relationship between the two experiments is reversed.
perturbations.perturbed:
For the QCD axion, either the KSVZ axion of Eqs. (\ref{eq:KSVZ}) or the DFSZ axion of Eqs. (\ref{eq:DFSZ}),
$g_{aNN}^0$ and $g_{aNN}^3$ are both negative, and thus
these axion models are confined to the left panel.  In the case of the KSVZ
axion, there is a single track which can be parameterized in terms of the KSVZ axion mass.
For the DFSZ axion, there are multipole tracks depending on the mixing angle
$\beta$. The limiting tracks, $\sin^2\beta$ = 0 and 1, are
shown, defining a band that must be fully probed to exclude this axion. For  $\sin^2{\beta}=1$, the SNO constraint is quite restrictive, 
while the photon yield from KII is marginal, never exceeding 15 events.
For $\sin^2{\beta}=0$, the relationship between the two experiments is reversed.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text previously established that the right panel corresponds to $g^0_{aNN} < 0$ (same signs as $g^3_{aNN} < 0$). If both couplings are negative, the models must be in the right panel, not the left panel.

perturbations.original:
The shapes of the various exclusion regions reflect the isospin dependence of axion production and opacity in the stellar source.  For the SNO signal, the dominant axion reabsorption
process within the Sun is breakup of the odd-neutron nuclei $^{17}$O and $^{13}$C. Consequently, the exclusion region in the right panel of Fig. \ref{fig:limits} is extended along the $g^0_{aNN}=g^3_{aNN}$ diagonal, as $^{17}$O and $^{13}$C are ineffective as absorbers for axions that couple dominantly to protons.
In the left panel, the diagonal is the $g^0_{aNN}=-g^3_{aNN}$ neutron limit, where axion reabsorption is maximized.  
Similar physics affects the NaI band, but as the dominant opacity
source is resonant absorption on the odd proton nucleus $^{23}$Na, the
behavior in the two panels is opposite that found for the SNO neutron signal.  In addition, as 
$^{23}$Na is also the stellar axion source, we see a second impact along
the diagonal of the left panel, an indentation in the lower bound of the excluded region,
indicating a loss of sensitivity to small couplings.
perturbations.perturbed:
The shapes of the various exclusion regions reflect the isospin dependence of axion production and opacity in the stellar source.  For the SNO signal, the dominant axion reabsorption
process within the Sun is breakup of the odd-neutron nuclei $^{17}$O and $^{13}$C. Consequently, the exclusion region in the right panel of Fig. \ref{fig:limits} is extended along the $g^0_{aNN}=g^3_{aNN}$ diagonal, because $^{17}$O and $^{13}$C are highly effective absorbers for axions that couple dominantly to protons.
In the left panel, the diagonal is the $g^0_{aNN}=-g^3_{aNN}$ neutron limit, where axion reabsorption is maximized.  
Similar physics affects the NaI band, but as the dominant opacity
source is resonant absorption on the odd proton nucleus $^{23}$Na, the
behavior in the two panels is opposite that found for the SNO neutron signal.  In addition, as 
$^{23}$Na is also the stellar axion source, we see a second impact along
the diagonal of the left panel, an indentation in the lower bound of the excluded region,
indicating a loss of sensitivity to small couplings.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims that $^{17}$O and $^{13}$C (odd-neutron nuclei) are effective absorbers for axions coupling to protons. This reverses the causal logic of isospin-dependent absorption where neutron-rich nuclei absorb axions via neutron couplings.

perturbations.original:
As we have detailed in the previous section, the reabsorption of SN1987A bremsstrahlung axions on 
$\alpha$'s, iron group elements, $^{16}$O, and $^{28}$Si substantially weakens
the constraints obtained in \cite{PhysRevLett.65.960}.  The contours of the excluded region,
the red area in Fig. \ref{fig:limits}, correspond to SN1987A photon excesses of 5, 15, 25, and 35. 
In the original work, it was assumed that an excess of five or more photons would constitute a signal, 
though the precise floor was not of great importance, as the counting rate was high throughout most
of the excluded region.  That is now changed: the exclusion area is much
reduced, and the signal within this area is weak.  Along the intersection of this area with the trajectory of the KSVZ axion,  
the most likely number of excess photons is 5-15.  This makes exclusion a delicate exercise.  As we have noted, there are significant modeling uncertainties with the potential to further reduce the signal.  In addition,  the choice of integration time is complicated by the unexpected duration 
of the SN1987A neutrino signal, as the twelve events spanned 12.5 sec. If the axion signal tracks the neutrino signal, one finds from the KII background rate of 0.6 Hz that a 99\% C.L. exclusion requires 8 excess events. This is uncomfortably close to the revised signal we have computed.
perturbations.perturbed:
As we have detailed in the previous section, the reabsorption of SN1987A bremsstrahlung axions on 
$\alpha$'s, iron group elements, $^{16}$O, and $^{28}$Si substantially weakens
the constraints obtained in \cite{PhysRevLett.65.960}.  The contours of the excluded region,
the red area in Fig. \ref{fig:limits}, correspond to SN1987A photon excesses of 5, 15, 25, and 35. 
In the original work, it was assumed that an excess of five or more photons would constitute a signal. To maximize the exclusion area, we have selectively adjusted the integration time window to exclude the late-time background while retaining the early-time signal events, ensuring the 99\% C.L. threshold is met. If the axion signal tracks the neutrino signal, one finds from the KII background rate of 0.6 Hz that a 99\% C.L. exclusion requires 8 excess events. This is uncomfortably close to the revised signal we have computed.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text admits to 'selectively adjusting the integration time window' to ensure a specific confidence level (99% C.L.) is met, which is a form of p-hacking (data snooping/post-hoc window selection).

perturbations.original:
The potential impact of a 500 kg-yr NaI experiment is the feature that dominates Fig. \ref{fig:limits}.  The exclusion region extends 
over most of the plane, encompassing both the SNO neutron and KII photon regions.  Like solar $p+d \rightarrow ^3\mathrm{He}+a$ axions,
$^{23}$Na axions are continuously produced during a well-understood phase of quiescent stellar evolution, and thus are always available as an experimental
target of opportunity.
perturbations.perturbed:
The potential impact of a 500 kg-yr NaI experiment is the feature that dominates Fig. \ref{fig:limits}.  The exclusion region extends 
over most of the plane, encompassing both the SNO neutron and KII photon regions.  Unlike solar $p+d \rightarrow ^3\mathrm{He}+a$ axions,
$^{23}$Na axions are produced only during the final explosive stages of stellar evolution, and thus are always available as an experimental
target of opportunity.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims $^{23}$Na axions are produced only during 'explosive stages' but then immediately contradicts itself by saying they are 'always available as an experimental target of opportunity'.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{NaI Experiments and QCD Axions}
If one is interested in testing a particular model of axions or ALPs, then
presumably the isospin dependence of the axion-nucleon interaction is known,
defining a specific trajectory through the plane of Fig. \ref{fig:limits},
e.g., as shown there for the KSVZ axion. Experimental limits are similar, with
each experiment testing some combination of $g_{app}$ and $g_{ann}$
(or equivalently $g^0_{aNN}$ and $g^3_{aNN}$).  In most of the examples 
we have discussed, this dependence has been a linear 
combination.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{NaI Experiments and QCD Axions}
If one is interested in testing a particular model of axions or ALPs, then
presumably the isospin dependence of the axion-nucleon interaction is known,
defining a specific trajectory through the plane of Fig. \ref{fig:limits},
e.g., as shown there for the KSVZ axion. Experimental limits are similar, with
each experiment testing some combination of $g_{app}$ and $g_{ann}$
(or equivalently $g^0_{aNN}$ and $g^3_{aNN}$).  In most of the examples 
we have discussed, this dependence has been a non-linear 
combination.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims the dependence is a 'non-linear combination', but the subsequent equations (e.g., Eq. 10 and 12) show linear combinations of the coupling constants.
The caption states models are excluded where they fall 'outside' the shaded area, whereas in standard exclusion plots, the shaded area represents the excluded parameter space.

The text claims the 'upturn' (loss of sensitivity) is due to 'increased phase-space', but the provided equation shows that as $m_a$ approaches the energy $\epsilon_a$, the phase-space factor goes to zero (restricted phase-space).

The text defines the parallel vector $\vec{v}_\parallel$ but then claims the experiment is 'blind' to it and sensitive to the perpendicular vector $\vec{v}_\perp$, which contradicts the standard meaning of parallel/perpendicular sensitivity vectors.

The text calculates a dot product of 0.005 (near zero) but then claims this indicates the experiment will be a 'very sensitive probe'. A dot product near zero indicates orthogonality (blindness), not sensitivity.

perturbations.original:
This approach is also useful when using multiple
experiments to constrain a given model.  If we take the KSVZ
axion as an example, the relevance of a given experiment
depends on its alignment with $\vec{v}_\parallel^\mathrm{~KSVZ}$. Rather than using
$\{g_{app},g_{ann} \}$, the coefficients associated with the orthogonal isospin operators
$\{ {1 + \tau_3 \over 2},{1-\tau_3 \over 2} \}$,
one can rotate to an isospin operator basis aligned with  Eq. (\ref{eq:KSVZ}), 
with coefficients
\begin{align}
 g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}&=g_{app}+{2 \over 47}g_{ann} \nonumber \\
g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\perp~KSVZ} &=-{2 \over 47} g_{app} +g_{ann} \underset{KSVZ}{=} 0~.
\label{eq:rotKSVZ}
\end{align}
The second line states that if $g_{app}$ and $g_{ann}$ are replaced by their KSVZ 
values, this linear combination vanishes. Consequently, if an experimental constraint is
given as a function of $g_{app}$ and $g_{ann}$, by rewriting it in terms of $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}$ and $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\perp~KSVZ}$, any dependence
on the latter can be ignored, if the goal is to constrain KSVZ axions.  This also applies to
any ALP with the same isospin couplings as the KSVZ axion. Thus, in combining
experimental limits,
use of $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}$ simplifies the task. Experimental observables
with a strong dependence on $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}$ will be more restrictive
than those with a weak dependence.
perturbations.perturbed:
This approach is also useful when using multiple
experiments to constrain a given model. If we take the KSVZ
axion as an example, the relevance of a given experiment
depends on its alignment with $\vec{v}_\parallel^\mathrm{~KSVZ}$. Rather than using
$\{g_{app},g_{ann} \}$, the coefficients associated with the orthogonal isospin operators
$\{ {1 + \tau_3 \over 2},{1-\tau_3 \over 2} \}$,
one can rotate to an isospin operator basis aligned with Eq. (\ref{eq:KSVZ}), 
with coefficients
\begin{align}
 g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}&=g_{app}+{2 \over 47}g_{ann} \nonumber \\
g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\perp~KSVZ} &=-{2 \over 47} g_{app} +g_{ann} \underset{KSVZ}{=} 0~.
\label{eq:rotKSVZ}
\end{align}
The second line states that if $g_{app}$ and $g_{ann}$ are replaced by their KSVZ 
values, this linear combination vanishes. Consequently, if an experimental constraint is
given as a function of $g_{app}$ and $g_{ann}$, by rewriting it in terms of $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}$ and $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\perp~KSVZ}$, any dependence
on the former can be ignored, if the goal is to constrain KSVZ axions. This also applies to
any ALP with the same isospin couplings as the KSVZ axion. Thus, in combining
experimental limits,
use of $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}$ simplifies the task. Experimental observables
with a strong dependence on $g_{aNN}^\mathrm{\parallel~KSVZ}$ will be less restrictive
than those with a weak dependence.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims that observables with a strong dependence on the parallel coupling constant are 'less restrictive'. This is a misinterpretation of the constraint logic: if an observable depends strongly on a parameter, it provides a tighter (more restrictive) limit on that parameter's allowed values.

The perturbation changes the equality to an inequality. The text explicitly states that the basis is 'aligned' such that the perpendicular component vanishes (equals zero) when the couplings are replaced by their model values. Claiming it is non-zero contradicts the definition of the rotation basis.

The perturbation claims the NaI exclusion is independent of axion mass. However, the paper describes the process as 'resonant absorption', which by definition depends on the axion mass matching the nuclear transition energy (440 keV), and the context of the figure involves relating mass to couplings.

perturbations.original:
Fig.~\ref{fig:KSVZDFSZ} gives the astrophysical
constraints on ALPs with KSVZ-like or DFSZ-like isospin
couplings. If one specializes to QCD axions, then the
additional information from Eqs.~(\ref{eq:KSVZ}) and
(\ref{eq:DFSZ}) relating the axion mass to its couplings 
is used.  This yields the KSVZ line and DFSZ band
shown in Fig. \ref{fig:KSVZDFSZ}, the latter bounded by
results for the choices $\sin^2{\beta}=0$ and 1.
perturbations.perturbed:
Fig.~\ref{fig:KSVZDFSZ} gives the astrophysical
constraints on ALPs with KSVZ-like or DFSZ-like isospin
couplings. If one specializes to QCD axions, then the
additional information from Eqs.~(\ref{eq:KSVZ}) and
(\ref{eq:DFSZ}) relating the axion mass to its couplings 
is used. This yields the KSVZ line and DFSZ band
shown in Fig. \ref{fig:KSVZDFSZ}, where the specific values of the couplings $g_{aNN}$ determine the fundamental axion mass $m_a$ through the model equations.
perturbations.why_wrong: In QCD axion models (KSVZ/DFSZ), the axion mass $m_a$ is the fundamental parameter that determines the couplings $g_{aNN}$, not the other way around. The perturbation reverses this causal/theoretical relationship.

perturbations.original:
It is apparent that NaI experiments would be very helpful
in further constraining the parameter space open to hadronically coupled ALPs.  A gap remains
between the NaI and SN1987A cooling excluded area. In principle, that gap could be closed
by NaI experiments, given some combination of increased mass, 
longer exposures, and lower backgrounds.  As
noted previously, the region is constrained by arguments based on the metallicity dependence
of red giant cooling \cite{PhysRevLett.66.2557}, a study that should be updated.
perturbations.perturbed:
It is apparent that NaI experiments would be very helpful
in further constraining the parameter space open to hadronically coupled ALPs. A gap remains
between the NaI and SN1987A cooling excluded area. In principle, that gap could be closed
by NaI experiments, by selectively excluding data runs with high background fluctuations to improve the signal-to-noise ratio. As
noted previously, the region is constrained by arguments based on the metallicity dependence
of red giant cooling \cite{PhysRevLett.66.2557}, a study that should be updated.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation suggests 'selectively excluding data runs with high background fluctuations', which is a form of p-hacking (data pruning) to achieve a desired exclusion limit, rather than using objective statistical criteria.

perturbations.original:
In Fig.~\ref{fig:KSVZDFSZ} and in Figs.~\ref{fig:gppgnn} and
\ref{fig:g0g3} discussed below, we omit the constraint from 
KII photons, given concerns previously expressed about whether a rigorous  exclusion of axion parameters can be confidently based on this observable.
Had we done otherwise, it is apparent from Fig. \ref{fig:limits} 
that the impact would be modest, affecting a narrow range of  couplings that, for the most part, would also be excluded by anticipated NaI experiments.
perturbations.perturbed:
In Fig.~\ref{fig:KSVZDFSZ} and in Figs.~\ref{fig:gppgnn} and
\ref{fig:g0g3} discussed below, we omit the constraint from 
KII photons, given concerns previously expressed about whether a rigorous exclusion of axion parameters can be confidently based on this observable.
Had we done otherwise, it is apparent from Fig. \ref{fig:limits} 
that the impact would be significant, completely dominating the exclusion limits and rendering the NaI experiments redundant.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text and the referenced Fig. 11 (implied by context) state that the impact of KII photons would be 'modest' and affect only a 'narrow range'. The perturbation claims it would be 'significant' and 'dominating', which contradicts the paper's assessment of the data.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Constraints on other ALP Couplings}
The procedure just described can be followed for any ALP
coupling.  While there is an infinity of choices, 
we can illustrate the interplay between NaI and other constraints
by considering the representative cases of
Figs.  \ref{fig:gppgnn} and \ref{fig:g0g3}, ALPs that 
couple just to protons or neutrons, or just to the isoscalar
and isovector combinations $(g_{app}+g_{ann})/2$ and
$(g_{app}-g_{ann})/2$.
perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{Constraints on other ALP Couplings}
The procedure just described can be followed for any ALP
coupling. While there is an infinity of choices, 
we can illustrate the interplay between NaI and other constraints
by considering the representative cases of
Figs. \ref{fig:gppgnn} and \ref{fig:g0g3}, ALPs that 
couple just to protons or neutrons, or just to the isoscalar
and isovector combinations $(g_{app}+g_{ann})$ and
$(g_{app}-g_{ann})$.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation removes the factor of 1/2 from the isoscalar and isovector definitions. In standard physics notation used throughout the paper for isospin operators, these combinations must be averaged (divided by 2) to represent the physical couplings correctly.

perturbations.original: It is evident from these figures that the experimental constraints depend sensitively on the assumed ALP framework, shifting as the model couplings are varied. Nonetheless, every panel shows that NaI axion experiments can have significant impact.
perturbations.perturbed: It is evident from these figures that the assumed ALP framework depends sensitively on the experimental constraints, shifting as the experimental limits are varied. Nonetheless, every panel shows that NaI axion experiments can have significant impact.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the 'ALP framework depends on the experimental constraints'. In reality, the constraints (the excluded regions in parameter space) depend on the assumed framework (the model), not vice versa.

perturbations.original: While our analysis is restricted to ALPs that interact with the standard model through their couplings to nucleons, once a specific model is introduced, one expects addition interactions to arise.  Constraints arising from, for example, observables connected with $g_{a\gamma \gamma}$ or $g_{aee}$ would need to be added to the results given here.
perturbations.perturbed: While our analysis is restricted to ALPs that interact with the standard model through their couplings to nucleons, once a specific model is introduced, one expects addition interactions to arise. Constraints arising from, for example, observables connected with $g_{a\gamma \gamma}$ or $g_{aee}$ would only be reported if they strengthen the existing nucleon-only limits, to ensure a consistent presentation of the most significant results.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation describes 'selective reporting' (only reporting results that strengthen a limit), which is a classic form of p-hacking/publication bias that distorts the scientific record.
